title: Ladenbergia bullata
taxonomy: Ladenbergia bullata is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. This classification places it within the order Gentianales and the clade Asterids. The family Rubiaceae is a large and diverse group of plants commonly known as the coffee or madder family. The Rubiaceae family is divided into several subfamilies, with Ladenbergia bullata belonging to the subfamily Cinchonoideae. Ladenbergia bullata is a member of the genus Ladenbergia, which is primarily found in tropical Africa.
uses: Ladenbergia bullata, also known as the golden satinflower, is a plant with potential medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used in various parts of the world to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. side_effects: Ladenbergia bullata, also known as Madagascar periwinkle, is often used in traditional medicine for various purposes. intro: Ladenbergia bullata is a plant species native to tropical regions, commonly known as the "Coral Plant" or "Pink Coral Plant" due to its vibrant pink flowers and coral-like inflorescences. The plant has been traditionally used in medicine, particularly in Asian cultures, to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, thanks to its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic properties. The bioactive constituents of Ladenbergia bullata include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Ladenbergia bullata is often prepared in herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as in traditional medicine formulas, to leverage its health benefits and therapeutic potential.
